meaning: To join, fasten, or connect one thing to another. | To fasten, join, or connect one thing to another.
example: Please attach the file to your email.
example2: He attached a note to the gift.
example3: The trailer is attached to the back of the truck.
Explanation: The verb 'attach' means to physically or digitally connect one item to another. It is frequently used in technology (attaching files), everyday objects (attaching a leash), and abstract concepts (attaching importance). The opposite is 'detach'. The preposition 'to' is almost always used after it. A common error is using 'connect' or 'join' where 'attach' is more specific for a secondary item being added to a main one. It implies a temporary or removable connection in many cases.
usageTip: Always use the preposition 'to' after attach (attach A to B).
commonMistake: Saying 'attach with' instead of the correct 'attach to'.

meaning: To join or connect one thing to another, or to add a file to an email
example: Don't forget to attach your resume to the email.
example2: You can attach a mouse to the laptop using a USB port.
example3: The app allows you to attach a photo to your message.
Explanation: To 'attach' is a very common digital action. It mostly refers to 'Email Attachments,' where you add a document or image to a message. It can also refer to physical hardware (attaching a printer) or software logic (attaching a debugger to a running program). It implies that two separate things are being linked together so they stay together. In web development, you might 'attach' an event listener to a button so it knows when it's clicked.
usageTip: Check the size of a file before you attach it to an email, as some systems have limits.
commonMistake: Saying 'attach a link'; usually, you 'insert' or 'paste' a link, but you 'attach' a file.
